title: What Makes ChocoNation Tick?
subtitle: Every business is a machine. Sort the parts of this one.

gameplay.scenario: ChocoNation is a young chocolate brand: an almond bar that costs ₹18 to make, sold through 40 kirana stores in your city. A business is a machine with parts that fit together — customers, the value you offer them, how you reach them, how money comes in, and what it costs to run. Sort each fact about ChocoNation into the part of the machine it belongs to.

buckets.label: Value Proposition
buckets.hint: WHY they choose you over alternatives

buckets.label: Channel
buckets.hint: HOW your product reaches the customer

buckets.label: Cost Structure
buckets.hint: WHAT it costs to run the machine

items.label: School and college students who want an affordable treat
items.explain: This describes WHO you serve. Notice it is specific — 'students who want an affordable treat', not 'everyone who likes chocolate'. Sharp customer segments make every other decision easier.

items.label: A crunchy almond centre no other bar at this price has
items.explain: This is WHY someone picks you over the big brand — your value proposition. It only counts if customers actually care about it and rivals don't offer it.

items.label: 40 kirana stores across the city
items.explain: Kirana stores are the path your bar travels to reach the customer's hand — a channel. Same product in a different channel (online, supermarkets) becomes almost a different business.

items.label: ₹30 collected every time a bar sells
items.explain: Money flowing IN from sales is a revenue stream. ChocoNation has just one; many businesses have several (think of a cinema: tickets AND popcorn).

items.label: ₹18 of cocoa, almonds, and wrapper per bar
items.explain: Costs that go INTO making each bar sit in the cost structure. Because these rise with every bar sold, founders call them 'direct' or 'variable' costs.

items.label: Monthly rent for the small production kitchen
items.explain: Also a cost — but notice it stays the same whether you sell 100 bars or 10,000. Fixed costs like rent are why selling MORE makes each bar effectively cheaper to produce.

items.label: Office workers looking for Diwali gift boxes
items.explain: A second WHO — a different customer segment with different needs (gifting, not snacking). Serving them well might need a different product, price, and even channel.

items.label: Delivering fresh stock to stores every Monday
items.explain: Distribution logistics are part of HOW the product reaches customers — the channel includes not just where you sell but how the product gets there reliably.

items.label: A ₹2,000 monthly fee from a cafe that stocks ChocoNation exclusively
items.explain: Money in, but from a different source than bar sales — a second revenue stream. Multiple streams make a business sturdier when one slows down.

items.label: Instagram ads to promote the new flavour
items.explain: Marketing spend is a cost — but unlike cocoa, it does not go into any single bar. Costs like ads, rent, and salaries are grouped as overheads (SG&A). You will meet them again in the Finance levels.
